title: Cholesteatoma
oneLiner: Keratinising squamous epithelial cyst in middle ear/mastoid; erodes bone, causes hearing loss, risks fatal intracranial infection.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Intracranial abscess (brain abscess) / meningitis** – the single finding that says "this is the killer": **focal neurological deficit** (hemiparesis, dysphasia) **OR** neck stiffness + photophobia.  
- **Sigmoid sinus thrombosis** – the single finding: **worsening headache + papilloedema** (raised ICP) without focal signs initially.  
- **Rule out these before assuming routine cholesteatoma** – any acute deterioration in consciousness, fever, or new focal signs mandates immediate CT head + neurosurgical review.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Foul-smelling, scanty, persistent aural discharge (often painless)
  - Conductive hearing loss (gradual, unilateral)
  - Aural fullness / pressure sensation
  - Tinnitus (less common)
  - Vertigo or imbalance (if labyrinthine erosion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Otoscopy: **retraction pocket** (posterosuperior quadrant of tympanic membrane), attic perforation, keratin debris (white pearly material)
  - May see **granulation tissue** or polyp in canal
  - Tuning fork tests: Rinne negative (conductive loss), Weber lateralises to affected ear
  - Check facial nerve function (CN VII) – weakness indicates advanced erosion
  - Cranial nerve exam if suspecting intracranial spread (III, IV, VI, VII, VIII)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM) without cholesteatoma** – no keratin debris, central perforation (vs attic/retraction pocket), granulations may be present but no retraction pocket.
  - **Otitis externa** – pain on tragus manipulation, canal swelling, no middle ear involvement.
  - **Middle ear effusion (glue ear)** – intact TM with fluid level, no retraction, no discharge.
  - **Foreign body** – history, unilateral non-infected debris.
  - **Granulomatous disease (e.g. Wegener's, TB)** – concurrent nasal, renal or systemic symptoms; biopsy required.
- **Splitter:** The single clinical feature that bifurcates cholesteatoma from simple CSOM is **presence of a retraction pocket or attic perforation containing keratin debris** – this is pathognomonic for cholesteatoma.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Otoscopy (ideally with microscope if available)
  - Tuning fork tests (Rinne, Weber)
  - Check facial nerve function
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR – elevated in acute infection/complication
  - Blood cultures if febrile (before antibiotics)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **CT temporal bone (high-resolution, non-contrast)** – shows bone erosion, soft tissue mass in middle ear/mastoid, labyrinthine or tegmen involvement.
  - **Second-line (if intracranial complication suspected):** **MRI brain with contrast** – better for abscess, sinus thrombosis, dural enhancement.
- **Special tests:**
  - Pure tone audiometry (conductive hearing loss ± sensorineural if advanced)
  - Videonystagmography (if vertigo – see if fistula present)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No validated score – diagnosis is clinical + imaging (CT typical findings).
- **Never-miss test:** **CT temporal bone** in all suspected cholesteatoma (before surgery, or if complications suspected). **If fever, headache, or neurological signs – do CT head + lumbar puncture** to rule out intracranial infection.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If **uncomplicated** (no fever, no neuro signs) – start **ototopical antibiotics** (e.g. ciprofloxacin + dexamethasone drops) **after aural toilet** (suction clean). DO NOT USE aminoglycoside drops (ototoxic).
  - If **complicated** (fever, headache, neck stiffness, focal neurology, facial palsy) – **IV broad-spectrum antibiotics** (e.g. ceftriaxone 2g IV once daily + metronidazole 500mg IV TDS) [check local policy] – cover for anaerobes and aerobes. **Urgent neurosurgical/ENT consultation**.
  - Give **analgesia** (paracetamol or ibuprofen) as needed.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if intracranial complication or significant infection – continue IV antibiotics, monitor GCS, NEWS2, neurology.
  - **Aural toilet** daily (microsuction under microscope by ENT team).
  - **CT temporal bone** (if not already done) for surgical planning.
  - **Audiometry** before any surgery.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Definitive treatment: surgical excision** – mastoidectomy (canal wall up or down) + reconstruction if needed. Refer to ENT.
  - Lifelong follow-up for recurrence (otoscopy + microsuction every 6-12 months) – high recurrence rate.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h if nil by mouth, otherwise maintain oral hydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** If immobile or neurological deficit – enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (check local policy for weight/renal adjustment).
  - **Diet/NBM:** Nil by mouth if surgery planned within 12-24h, otherwise normal diet.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2, neuro observations (GCS, pupils, limb power) if any intracranial concern – increase to 1-hourly if deteriorating.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Ototopical:** Ciprofloxacin + dexamethasone drops (e.g. Cilodex) – 1-2 drops into affected ear twice daily for 7-10 days (after aural toilet).
  - **Systemic antibiotics (if infection or complication):**
    - Co-amoxiclav: 625mg PO TDS (mild) or 1.2g IV TDS (severe) – **or**
    - Ceftriaxone 2g IV once daily + metronidazole 500mg IV TDS (if penicillin allergy or severe intracranial infection) [check local policy].
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO (if mild infection and no penicillin allergy – less anaerobic cover).
  - Clarithromycin 500mg BD PO (if penicillin allergic).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: increased risk of diarrhoea/Clostridioides difficile. Avoid if penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis).
  - Aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin, neomycin) – **contraindicated** in cholesteatoma (ototoxic, may cause sensorineural hearing loss).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment in mild-to-moderate renal impairment; caution in severe (eGFR <10).
  - Co-amoxiclav: adjust dose if eGFR <30 (prolonged interval).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Using aminoglycoside ear drops** (e.g. gentamicin) – this is **ototoxic** and can cause irreversible hearing loss. Do not prescribe in any patient with tympanic membrane perforation / retraction pocket.
  - **Giving IV antibiotics without first sending blood cultures** – you lose the chance to identify the pathogen in a complicated case.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Mastoiditis (pain, swelling over mastoid, pinna displaced)
  - Facial nerve palsy (sudden or gradual – seek urgent surgical decompression)
  - Labyrinthitis (acute vertigo, nystagmus, sensorineural hearing loss)
  - Intracranial abscess (brain abscess / extradural abscess) – headache, fever, neuro deficit
  - Meningitis – fever, neck stiffness, photophobia
  - Sigmoid sinus thrombosis – severe headache, papilloedema, seizures
- **Chronic:**
  - Conductive hearing loss (persistent even after surgery)
  - Recurrent cholesteatoma (erosion of ossicles, labyrinth, tegmen)
  - Labyrinthine fistula (vertigo with pressure change, e.g. Valsalva)
  - Chronic mastoiditis and suppurative otitis
sections.Prognosis: - Complete surgical excision yields good prognosis with resolution of infection and hearing stabilisation. Recurrence rate 5-10% (higher if canal wall up procedure). Lifelong follow-up essential. Untreated, leads to progressive hearing loss, facial palsy, and fatal intracranial infection (abscess, meningitis).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always refer all cholesteatoma to ENT** – it is a surgical disease; medical management is only temporising.
- **Perform a full cranial nerve exam** (especially VII, VIII, and lower CNs) in any patient with cholesteatoma and headache or fever – early detection of intracranial spread saves lives.
- **Order CT temporal bone** before surgical referral – it helps the ENT team plan the approach and detects hidden bone erosion.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT use aminoglycoside ear drops** (gentamicin, neomycin) – they are ototoxic behind a retraction pocket/perforation.
- **Do NOT assume all foul-smelling ear discharge is otitis externa** – consider cholesteatoma and examine the tympanic membrane (may be hidden behind debris; microsuction needed).
- **Do NOT delay surgery in complicated cases** – intracranial complications require urgent drainage/decompression; waiting for "stabilisation" can be fatal.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening headache, fever, neck stiffness
  - Sudden facial weakness or vertigo
  - Worsening hearing loss or change in discharge (blood-stained)
- **Follow-up:**
  - ENT outpatient clinic within **2 weeks** for surgical planning.
  - If no surgery – repeat otoscopy + microscopic cleaning every 3-6 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ENT reg / neurosurgery / ICU if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, conscious level)
  - **GCS < 13** or any drop in GCS
  - **New focal neurological sign** (e.g., CN VII palsy, limb weakness)
  - **Seizure** or suspected brain abscess
  - **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** (if blood gas done) with signs of sepsis
  - **Refractory headache** with papilloedema – suspect sinus thrombosis
- **Immediate senior review (within 30 minutes)** for any of the above.

rawMarkdown:
## Cholesteatoma
**One-liner:** Keratinising squamous epithelial cyst in middle ear/mastoid; erodes bone, causes hearing loss, risks fatal intracranial infection.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Intracranial abscess (brain abscess) / meningitis** – the single finding that says "this is the killer": **focal neurological deficit** (hemiparesis, dysphasia) **OR** neck stiffness + photophobia.  
- **Sigmoid sinus thrombosis** – the single finding: **worsening headache + papilloedema** (raised ICP) without focal signs initially.  
- **Rule out these before assuming routine cholesteatoma** – any acute deterioration in consciousness, fever, or new focal signs mandates immediate CT head + neurosurgical review.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Foul-smelling, scanty, persistent aural discharge (often painless)
  - Conductive hearing loss (gradual, unilateral)
  - Aural fullness / pressure sensation
  - Tinnitus (less common)
  - Vertigo or imbalance (if labyrinthine erosion)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Otoscopy: **retraction pocket** (posterosuperior quadrant of tympanic membrane), attic perforation, keratin debris (white pearly material)
  - May see **granulation tissue** or polyp in canal
  - Tuning fork tests: Rinne negative (conductive loss), Weber lateralises to affected ear
  - Check facial nerve function (CN VII) – weakness indicates advanced erosion
  - Cranial nerve exam if suspecting intracranial spread (III, IV, VI, VII, VIII)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely:**
  - **Chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM) without cholesteatoma** – no keratin debris, central perforation (vs attic/retraction pocket), granulations may be present but no retraction pocket.
  - **Otitis externa** – pain on tragus manipulation, canal swelling, no middle ear involvement.
  - **Middle ear effusion (glue ear)** – intact TM with fluid level, no retraction, no discharge.
  - **Foreign body** – history, unilateral non-infected debris.
  - **Granulomatous disease (e.g. Wegener's, TB)** – concurrent nasal, renal or systemic symptoms; biopsy required.
- **Splitter:** The single clinical feature that bifurcates cholesteatoma from simple CSOM is **presence of a retraction pocket or attic perforation containing keratin debris** – this is pathognomonic for cholesteatoma.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Otoscopy (ideally with microscope if available)
  - Tuning fork tests (Rinne, Weber)
  - Check facial nerve function
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, ESR – elevated in acute infection/complication
  - Blood cultures if febrile (before antibiotics)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **CT temporal bone (high-resolution, non-contrast)** – shows bone erosion, soft tissue mass in middle ear/mastoid, labyrinthine or tegmen involvement.
  - **Second-line (if intracranial complication suspected):** **MRI brain with contrast** – better for abscess, sinus thrombosis, dural enhancement.
- **Special tests:**
  - Pure tone audiometry (conductive hearing loss ± sensorineural if advanced)
  - Videonystagmography (if vertigo – see if fistula present)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No validated score – diagnosis is clinical + imaging (CT typical findings).
- **Never-miss test:** **CT temporal bone** in all suspected cholesteatoma (before surgery, or if complications suspected). **If fever, headache, or neurological signs – do CT head + lumbar puncture** to rule out intracranial infection.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If **uncomplicated** (no fever, no neuro signs) – start **ototopical antibiotics** (e.g. ciprofloxacin + dexamethasone drops) **after aural toilet** (suction clean). DO NOT USE aminoglycoside drops (ototoxic).
  - If **complicated** (fever, headache, neck stiffness, focal neurology, facial palsy) – **IV broad-spectrum antibiotics** (e.g. ceftriaxone 2g IV once daily + metronidazole 500mg IV TDS) [check local policy] – cover for anaerobes and aerobes. **Urgent neurosurgical/ENT consultation**.
  - Give **analgesia** (paracetamol or ibuprofen) as needed.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit if intracranial complication or significant infection – continue IV antibiotics, monitor GCS, NEWS2, neurology.
  - **Aural toilet** daily (microsuction under microscope by ENT team).
  - **CT temporal bone** (if not already done) for surgical planning.
  - **Audiometry** before any surgery.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Definitive treatment: surgical excision** – mastoidectomy (canal wall up or down) + reconstruction if needed. Refer to ENT.
  - Lifelong follow-up for recurrence (otoscopy + microsuction every 6-12 months) – high recurrence rate.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1L over 8h if nil by mouth, otherwise maintain oral hydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** If immobile or neurological deficit – enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (check local policy for weight/renal adjustment).
  - **Diet/NBM:** Nil by mouth if surgery planned within 12-24h, otherwise normal diet.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2, neuro observations (GCS, pupils, limb power) if any intracranial concern – increase to 1-hourly if deteriorating.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Ototopical:** Ciprofloxacin + dexamethasone drops (e.g. Cilodex) – 1-2 drops into affected ear twice daily for 7-10 days (after aural toilet).
  - **Systemic antibiotics (if infection or complication):**
    - Co-amoxiclav: 625mg PO TDS (mild) or 1.2g IV TDS (severe) – **or**
    - Ceftriaxone 2g IV once daily + metronidazole 500mg IV TDS (if penicillin allergy or severe intracranial infection) [check local policy].
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS PO (if mild infection and no penicillin allergy – less anaerobic cover).
  - Clarithromycin 500mg BD PO (if penicillin allergic).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: increased risk of diarrhoea/Clostridioides difficile. Avoid if penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis).
  - Aminoglycoside drops (gentamicin, neomycin) – **contraindicated** in cholesteatoma (ototoxic, may cause sensorineural hearing loss).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment in mild-to-moderate renal impairment; caution in severe (eGFR <10).
  - Co-amoxiclav: adjust dose if eGFR <30 (prolonged interval).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Using aminoglycoside ear drops** (e.g. gentamicin) – this is **ototoxic** and can cause irreversible hearing loss. Do not prescribe in any patient with tympanic membrane perforation / retraction pocket.
  - **Giving IV antibiotics without first sending blood cultures** – you lose the chance to identify the pathogen in a complicated case.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Mastoiditis (pain, swelling over mastoid, pinna displaced)
  - Facial nerve palsy (sudden or gradual – seek urgent surgical decompression)
  - Labyrinthitis (acute vertigo, nystagmus, sensorineural hearing loss)
  - Intracranial abscess (brain abscess / extradural abscess) – headache, fever, neuro deficit
  - Meningitis – fever, neck stiffness, photophobia
  - Sigmoid sinus thrombosis – severe headache, papilloedema, seizures
- **Chronic:**
  - Conductive hearing loss (persistent even after surgery)
  - Recurrent cholesteatoma (erosion of ossicles, labyrinth, tegmen)
  - Labyrinthine fistula (vertigo with pressure change, e.g. Valsalva)
  - Chronic mastoiditis and suppurative otitis

### Prognosis
- Complete surgical excision yields good prognosis with resolution of infection and hearing stabilisation. Recurrence rate 5-10% (higher if canal wall up procedure). Lifelong follow-up essential. Untreated, leads to progressive hearing loss, facial palsy, and fatal intracranial infection (abscess, meningitis).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always refer all cholesteatoma to ENT** – it is a surgical disease; medical management is only temporising.
- **Perform a full cranial nerve exam** (especially VII, VIII, and lower CNs) in any patient with cholesteatoma and headache or fever – early detection of intracranial spread saves lives.
- **Order CT temporal bone** before surgical referral – it helps the ENT team plan the approach and detects hidden bone erosion.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT use aminoglycoside ear drops** (gentamicin, neomycin) – they are ototoxic behind a retraction pocket/perforation.
- **Do NOT assume all foul-smelling ear discharge is otitis externa** – consider cholesteatoma and examine the tympanic membrane (may be hidden behind debris; microsuction needed).
- **Do NOT delay surgery in complicated cases** – intracranial complications require urgent drainage/decompression; waiting for "stabilisation" can be fatal.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New or worsening headache, fever, neck stiffness
  - Sudden facial weakness or vertigo
  - Worsening hearing loss or change in discharge (blood-stained)
- **Follow-up:**
  - ENT outpatient clinic within **2 weeks** for surgical planning.
  - If no surgery – repeat otoscopy + microscopic cleaning every 3-6 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ENT reg / neurosurgery / ICU if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, conscious level)
  - **GCS < 13** or any drop in GCS
  - **New focal neurological sign** (e.g., CN VII palsy, limb weakness)
  - **Seizure** or suspected brain abscess
  - **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** (if blood gas done) with signs of sepsis
  - **Refractory headache** with papilloedema – suspect sinus thrombosis
- **Immediate senior review (within 30 minutes)** for any of the above.
